name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقُولُوا۟ رَٰعِنَا وَقُولُوا۟ ٱنظُرْنَا وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١٠٤)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not say Ra‘ina, but say Unzurna, and listen! And there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (104)
```

```arabic
مَّا يَوَدُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَلَا ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ أَن يُنَزَّلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١٠٥)
```

```english
Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book nor the idolaters like that any good be showered on you from your Lord; but Allah singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and Allah is dispenser of a mighty grace. (105)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أيها الذين آمنوا** (O you who have faith): This is the first instance in the Quran where the believers are addressed with the expression "O you who have faith" (يا أيها الذين آمنوا). This form of address occurs in the Quran in approximately eighty-five places. Referring to the believers with the phrase "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا)—whether in the form of a direct address or otherwise—is something exclusive to this nation (الأمة). As for previous nations, they were referred to by the term "people" (القوم), such as His saying: **قَوْمَ نُوحٍ أَوْ قَوْمَ هُودٍ** — "the people of Noah or the people of Hud" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-A'raf, Ayah 59 & 65], and His saying: **قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِن كُنتُ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ** — "He said, ‘O my people! Tell me, should I stand on a manifest proof...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Hud, Ayah 63], and His saying: **اصحاب مدين وأصحاب الرس** — "the inhabitants of Midian and the inhabitants of Rass" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Hajj, Ayah 44 & Surah Al-Furqan, Ayah 38], and "the Children of Israel" (بني إسرائيل) and "O Children of Israel" (يا بني إسرائيل). Thus, the expression "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) is an honorific title (التشرف) exclusive to this nation.

However, contemplation (التدبر) on His speech, the Exalted, reveals that the expression "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) in His discourse is intended to mean something different from the term "the believers" (المؤمنون)—such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَتُوبُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — "and rally to Allah in repentance, O faithful" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nur, Ayah 31]—in terms of its referent (المصداق). The Exalted says: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنَا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ رَّحْمَةً وَعِلْمًا فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ وَقِهِمْ عَذَابَ الْجَحِيمِ ، رَبَّنَا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدتَّهُمْ وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَأَزْوَاجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Those who bear the Throne, and those who are around it, celebrate the praise of their Lord and have faith in Him, and they plead for forgiveness for the faithful: ‘Our Lord, You embrace all things in mercy and knowledge! So forgive those who repent and follow Your way, and save them from the punishment of hell. Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses and their descendants. Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Ghafir, Ayah 7-8].

He made the seeking of forgiveness by the angels and the bearers of the Throne first for "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا), then He substituted it a second time with His saying: "for those who repent and follow" (للذين تابوا واتبعوا), and repentance (التوبة) is the act of returning (الرجوع). Then He linked their supplication to "those who have faith" and joined to them their fathers and descendants. If those described as "those who have faith" were simply all the people of faith in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, regardless of their status, then "those who have faith" would have encompassed everyone—fathers, children, and spouses—and there would have been no place for the conjunction (العطف) or the distinction; everyone would have been on the same level and placed in a single rank.

This meaning is also derived from His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَانٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَا أَلَتْنَاهُم مِّن/ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَيْءٍ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — "As for those who have faith and whose descendants followed them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds. Every person is a hostage to what he has earned." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah At-Tur, Ayah 21]. If their descendants who followed them in faith were themselves the referents (مصداقاً) of "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) in His speech, there would be no reason for "uniting" (الإلحاق) them. Furthermore, if His saying "and whose descendants followed them" (وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم) were a contextual clue (قرينة) indicating specific individuals from "those who have faith"—namely every group of believers in relation to their believing descendants—there would still be no reason for the "uniting," nor would there be a correct justification for His saying "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds," except in the case of the final generation who have no descendants to follow them in faith and are thus united with their fathers. While this is a conceivable meaning, the context (سياق) of the verse—which is a context of bestowing honor (التشريف)—rejects this. Under that interpretation, the meaning would revert to something like our saying: "The believers are from one another" or "some are united with others," where they are all in one rank without any superiority (شرافة) of some over others, nor of the predecessor over the successor, since the criterion is faith (الإيمان), which is the same in all of them. This contradicts the context of the verse, which indicates a kind of nobility (كرامة) and honor for the predecessor by uniting his descendants with him.

Thus, His saying "and whose descendants followed them in faith" is a contextual clue indicating specific individuals by His saying "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا); they are the foremost pioneers (السابقون الأولون) in faith in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم from the Emigrants (المهاجرين) and the Helpers (الأنصار) during the days of hardship. Therefore, the phrase "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) is a word of honor (كلمة تشريف) intended for these individuals. This is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted: **لِلْفُقَرَاءِ الْمُهَاجِرِينَ** — "[They are also] for the poor Emigrants..." until He says: **وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّءُوا الدَّارَ وَالْإِيمَانَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ** — "And [also for] those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them..." until He says: **وَالَّذِينَ جَاءُوا مِن بَعْدِهِمْ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لَنَا وَلِإِخْوَانِنَا الَّذِينَ سَبَقُونَا بِالْإِيمَانِ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ فِي قُلُوبِنَا غِلًّا لِّلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا رَبَّنَا إِنَّكَ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "And [also for] those who came after them, who say, ‘Our Lord, forgive us and our brethren who preceded us in faith, and do not put any rancour in our hearts toward the faithful. Our Lord, You are indeed most kind and merciful.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Hashr, Ayah 8-10]. If the referent (مصداق) of His saying "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) were identical to the referent of His saying "those who preceded us in faith" (الذين سبقونا بالإيمان), it would be a case of placing a manifest noun (الظاهر) in the place of a pronoun (المضمر) without an apparent reason.

What has been mentioned is also suggested by His saying, the Exalted: **مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَاءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَرَاهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانًا** — "Muhammad, the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in worship], seeking Allah’s grace and [His] pleasure..." until He says: **وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا** — "Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Fath, Ayah 29].

In summary, the phrase is a word of honor (كلمة تشريف) exclusive to the foremost pioneers among the believers. It is not unlikely that a similar reasoning applies to the phrase "those who disbelieve" (الذين كفروا), referring to the foremost in disbelief in the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم from the polytheists of Mecca and their peers, as suggested by verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ** — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 6].

**If you say:** Based on what has passed, the address to "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) would be restricted to a specific number of those present during the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم, whereas the scholars have mentioned that these are general addresses for the time of presence and otherwise, for those present in the era of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم and others, especially based on the approach of treating the address as a real proposition (قضية حقيقية).

**I say:** Yes, it is an honorific address (خطاب تشريفي) that is specific to some, but that does not necessitate that the legal obligations (التكاليف) contained within the address are restricted to them. The breadth or narrowness of a legal obligation has causes other than those that necessitate the breadth or narrowness of an address. Just as obligations devoid of a direct address are general and broad without an address, the introduction of some obligations with the address "O you who have faith" (يا ايها الذين آمنوا) is like the introduction of other addresses with the phrase "O Prophet" (يا أيها النبي) or "O Messenger" (يا أيها الرسول), based on honor (التشريف), while the obligation is general and the intent is broad. Despite all this, the honorific exclusivity we mentioned does not prevent the term "those who have faith" (الذين آمنوا) from being applied to others besides those specifically honored, provided there is a contextual clue (قرينة) indicating that, such as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ آمَنُوا ثُمَّ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ ازْدَادُوا كُفْرًا لَّمْ يَكُنِ اللَّهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ** — "As for those who believe and then disbelieve, then believe [again] and then disbelieve and then increase in their disbelief, Allah shall never forgive them..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nisa, Ayah 137], and His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Noah: **وَمَا أَنَا بِطَارِدِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّهُم مُّلَاقُو رَبِّهِمْ** — "and I will not drive away those who have faith; they will indeed encounter their Lord..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Hud, Ayah 29].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا تقولوا راعنا وقولوا أنظرنا** (Do not say ‘Ra‘ina,’ but say ‘Unzurna’): That is, replace the word *ra‘ina* (راعنا) with the word *unzurna* (انظرنا); and if you do not do so, it will be an act of disbelief (كفر) from you, and for the disbelievers is a painful punishment. Thus, it contains a severe prohibition against saying *ra‘ina*. This is a word mentioned in another verse which explained its meaning in general, namely His saying, the Exalted: **مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَاسْمَعْ غَيْرَ مُسْمَعٍ وَرَاعِنَا لَيًّا بِأَلْسِنَتِهِمْ وَطَعْنًا فِي الدِّينِ** — "Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings, and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Listen without being listened to!’ and ‘Ra‘ina,’ twisting their tongues and reviling the religion." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah An-Nisa, Ayah 46]. From this, it is known that the Jews intended by their saying to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم *ra‘ina* something similar to the meaning of His saying: "Listen without being listened to" (اسمع غير مسمع). For this reason, the prohibition was issued against addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with that.

At that point, it aligns with what has been narrated: that the Muslims used to address the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with that when he delivered a speech to them, saying, "Observe us (*ra‘ina*), O Messenger of Allah"—intending "give us time and look at us (*unthurna*) so that we may understand what you say." However, the word in the language of the Jews carried the meaning of an insult. The Jews seized this opportunity and began addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم with it, appearing to be polite with him while intending the insult; its meaning to them was "Listen, may you not hear." Thus, it was revealed: "Among the Jews are those who pervert words from their meanings, and say, ‘We hear and disobey’ and ‘Listen without being listened to!’ and ‘Ra‘ina’..." the verse. Allah prohibited the believers from this word and commanded them to say what carries its intended meaning, which is "look at us" (*unzurna*), so He said: **لا تقولوا راعنا وتقولوا أنظرنا** — "Do not say ‘Ra‘ina,’ but say ‘Unzurna’."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وللكافرين عذاب أليم** (and for the faithless there is a painful punishment): He intends those who rebel against this prohibition. This is one of the instances where disbelief (الكفر) is applied to the abandonment of secondary legal obligations (التكاليف الفرعية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما يود الذين كفروا من أهل الكتاب** (Neither the faithless from among the People of the Book...): If the intent by "People of the Book" (أهل الكتاب) is the Jews specifically—as is apparent since the preceding addresses were directed at them—then describing them as "People of the Book" serves to point to the cause (العلة). That is, because they are People of a Book, they do not wish for the Book to be revealed to the believers, as that would necessitate the end of their exclusive status as the people of the Book. This is despite the fact that such an attitude is a form of stinginess (ضنّة) regarding something they do not own, and an opposition to Allah, the Glorified, in the vastness of His mercy and the greatness of His grace. If the intent is the People of the Book in general—both Jews and Christians—then it is a generalization after specification, due to the two groups sharing certain traits and their shared resentment toward Islam. This view may be supported by some subsequent verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا لَن يَدْخُلَ الْجَنَّةَ إِلَّا مَن كَانَ هُودًا أَوْ نَصَارَىٰ** — "And they say, ‘No one will enter paradise except one who is a Jew or a Christian.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 111], and His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ لَيْسَتِ النَّصَارَىٰ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَىٰ لَيْسَتِ الْيَهُودُ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ وَهُمْ يَتْلُونَ الْكِتَابَ** — "The Jews say, ‘The Christians stand on nothing,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Jews stand on nothing,’ though they follow the [same] Book." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 113].

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Nu'aym narrated in *al-Hilyah* from Ibn Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم said: "Allah did not reveal any verse containing 'O you who have faith' (يا ايها الذين آمنوا) except that Ali is its head and its leader."

**I say:** The narration supports what we will mention of the narrations regarding several verses stating that they are about Ali or the People of the House (أهل البيت), similar to what is in His saying, the Exalted: **كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ** — "You are the best nation [ever] brought forth for mankind..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Imran, Ayah 110], and His saying, the Exalted: **لِّتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ** — "so that you may be witnesses to mankind..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayah 143], and His saying, the Exalted: **وَكُونُوا مَعَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — "and be with the truthful" [QARAI TRANSLATION: Surah At-Tawbah, Ayah 119].
